entry.title: SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues
entry.content:
Are you interested in activating Joule and integrating it seamlessly into your system—but running into errors along the way? In my role as solution architect for our SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group I’ve worked with some of our first Joule customers and would like to share how you can avoid and resolve errors during the activation.

This blog highlights the most common SAP Joule issues encountered during provisioning, booster execution, and system integration.

You’ll also find clear, step-by-step guidance to help you quickly troubleshoot and succeed with your activation.
Please also refer to our SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule Health Check blog to test your activation and configuration is working correctly before using Joule https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-and-joule-health-checks/ba-p/14348321

We will look at the following topics:

  1. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) subaccount / provisioning issues
  2. Joule booster problems
  3. User Experience (UX) / icon appearance issues
  4. SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning service (IPS) and Identity Authentication service (IAS) issues
  5. Restrictions/ constraints
  6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
  7. References
  8. How to contact SAP Support

1. SAP BTP subaccount / provisioning issues

Issue

Solution/Available fix

You have a license for an SAP product that supports Joule integration (such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, etc.) and after creating a subaccount the Joule service is not available in the subaccount entitlement.

Check SAP Help:Data Centers Supported by Joule

The data centre is supported but the entitlements are not available in your global account.

Contact your SAP Account Manager/Executive. 

Refer to SAP Note 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Help:Joule prerequisites

The menu entry "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of your SAP BTP Cockpit.

You are not an administrator of the Global account.

SAP Note 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" is not visible in the Global account of SAP BTP Cockpit

SAP Note 2669325 - How to add a user as a SAP Business Technology Platform Global Account Administrator

SAP systems are not visible in SAP BTP under the system landscape.

 

 

Before adding your system, make sure it's not already auto-discovered and listed automatically on the System Landscape ---> Systems page.

Refer to SAP Help: BTP Registering SAP System
SAP Note 3661817 - SAP systems are missing in System Landscape of the BTP Cockpit

 2. Joule Activation/Issues during the execution of the Joule booster

While running the booster configuration for the Joule Service, the prerequisites check failed for the Identity Authentication Tenant.

 An error message is displayed: "Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant."

 

Ensure that the IAS tenant is visible under the global account and configure trust between the Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and the Joule subaccount. 

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

 

 

 

The Joule booster is being run, and you encounter the following error:

*********************************
Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host <IAS tenant>. 
Report an incident in the BC-CP-MP component 

The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant isn't connected to the correct SAP BTP Global Account, or it's linked to the wrong customer ID.
How to Fix it:
a. Make sure your IAS tenant is linked to the correct Global Account.
Check the SAP Help:View Assigned Tenants and Admins.

b. If you have multiple IAS tenants , use the IAS tenant that matches the Global Account's customer ID.
Important:Data syncing between systems usually takes up to one week.Wait for this to complete before running the booster again so that the SAP IAS system appears in all the accounts.

You can manually trigger the sync via the “Refresh” button.
SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

Enabling Joule leads to the following error: 
"Prerequisites for the formation type "Integration with Joule" are not fulfilled.
No SAP Cloud identity service system is showing up under“System landscape” --->System

 

A new SAP BTP Global Account will take some time to synchronize all existing customer systems, but you can manually trigger the sync via the "Refresh" button.
This should ensure that your SAP Cloud Identity Services system shows up. Afterwards you can proceed with the Joule configuration.

 

The SAP Build Work Zone system does not appear in the dropdown list during the setup process (Joule Booster - Step 5 Set Up Integrations).

 

 

The SAP Build Work Zone service was not subscribed to under the relevant subaccount.
An instance of this service is not enough for the integration; therefore, the system will not appear under the options.

Add the SAP Build Work Zone subscription in the Joule subaccount. Refer to
SAP Note 3710864 - SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

Unable to find the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition option in Step 3 - "Select Integration" stage dropdown during activation by means of the Joule Booster.

In global account Systems, the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system is already auto-discovered.

Ensure that it is visible in Joule Booster Step 04  "Select Capabilities", select "S/4HANA Private Cloud" and proceed to complete the Joule booster.

Refer to SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster for detailed information.

Error message: "The subscription of subaccount <xxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxx> to application das-application and plan foundation, failed with state: SUBSCRIBE_FAILED."

Observe the error messages indicating SaaS Subscription failed and instructions to open a support case or unsubscribe and retry.

 

Refer to SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster for detailed instructions.

If the issue persists, raise a support incident and choose component CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB for further investigation.

 

 

 3. UX/ Icon appearance issue

Joule refused to frame 'https://xxx.xxx.cloud.sap/' because an ancestor violates the following Content Security Policy directive: "frame-ancestors”.                             

 

 

 

 

Enable 3rd party cookies of your browser.Add the trusted domain in SAP IAS and SAP BTP
a. Add trusted domain in SAP IAS in the SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
Go to Applications & Resources -->Tenant Settings --> Trusted Domains--> Click the + (Add) button
Type the URL of the trusted host into the field.

b. Add the trusted domain in SAP BTP.
In SAP BTP cockpit, navigate to your specific subaccount ---> Security ---> Settings.
Click the + (Add) button.
Enter the required hostname, for example: https://<your-s4-or-sfsf-url>.com

The Joule icon at the top app bar in the Fiori Launchpad is not properly displayed.

The issue is caused using an unsupported SAPUI5 version in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system. 

 

Upgrade the SAPUI5 Component version of SAP S/4HANA to be equal to or higher than the minimum supported version.

SAP Notes:
2346367 - How to upgrade the SAPUI5 library in ABAP - SAP for Me
3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
 

After integrating Joule with SAP Solutions, the user is unable to launch Joule by clicking the diamond icon,  it fails to load or respond.

 

 

Allow third-party cookies.

SAP Note 3643889 - User unable to launch the Joule Icon after integrating with SAP Solutions

The Joule icon is not appearing for a specific user despite that the correct role-based permissions have been assigned in SAP SuccessFactors.

The issue is most likely caused by security policies of the network provider, which may be blocking certain URLs that are required for the icon to appear.
The affected user does not have the General User Permission --->Access to Joule

Refer to SAP Note 3685621 - Joule button not appearing on SuccessFactors

After clicking on the Joule icon in SAP SuccessFactors, it tries to load for a few minutes and subsequently the user gets a blank or greyed-out screen.

Refer to SAP Note 3544482 - Getting Error / White /Blank / IAS login Screen When Trying to Launch Joule in SuccessFactors

4. SAP IPS/IAS issues

The SAP IAS source system read job is failing.

 

Keep the source system name the same as in the content channel that had been created in the standard SAP Build Work Zone.

Joule was successfully integrated with the SAP product. However, clicking the Joule icon results in a blank white or SAP IAS login screen. Single sign-on is not working.

 

Check the SAP IAS domain. In the SAP BTP Joule subaccount, go to Trust Configuration and select the Custom Identity Provider for Applications.

Change the field "Domain" to the one used in the SAP product.

Refer to SAP Note 3635949 - Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

5. Restrictions /Constraints
SAP Joule operates within specific constraints.
SAP notes:
3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
3711505 - Restrictions: Multiple different Work Zone editions integration with Joule
Before starting the Joule project, please refer to the document below. All constraints applicable to Joule are listed here.
SAP Help:Joule constraints

6. Monitoring and troubleshooting
You may experience issues that would have to be reported in SAP incidents. In such cases, please collect information, which will help the support team to investigate and resolve the problem quickly.
SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Jouletroubleshooting
SAP Help:Monitoring and Troubleshooting

7. How to report issues
You can report an incident or error through SAP for Me. Ensure that you provide a detailed description of the issue including the steps to reproduce it. Attach any relevant screenshots, logs, or error messages to help with the investigation. Please use the appropriate components listed below when creating your incident.

General support component for core Joule services issue and functionality

CA_JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

SaaS Subscription error after running Joule Booster

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

8. Frequently asked questions

  1. Are there options available to customize the theme of Joule so it aligns with our branding guidelines and specific colour palette?

The theme tab is only available when Joule is used in a standalone (single page) mode. For now, Joule automatically adapts to the theme of the embedding application.  For example, if the user will changes the theme in the SAP Work Zone settings, Joule will automatically adopt it. The user cannot choose a different theme for the Joule client.
SAP Note 3646989 - Is it possible to adjust theming for Joule?

  1. Is it possible to remove the Joule icon for selected users?

When Joule is enabled through the Joule booster, it is activated for all users in both SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems. At present, there is no supported option to restrict or disable Joule for specific users or user groups.
SAP Note 3698807 - How to Remove or Hide the Joule Icon in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Summary
In this blog, we reviewed common configuration and activation issues related to Joule including challenges with SAP Business Technology Platform provisioning, Joule booster activation, UX visibility, and integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services (IPS/IAS), along with key restrictions, monitoring tips, and reference resources.

In addition, we discussed current system restrictions and limitations, monitoring options, log analysis techniques, and practical troubleshooting steps to help identify and resolve issues efficiently.

By following the guidance and best practices outlined above, administrators can streamline Joule activation, minimize configuration errors, and ensure a smoother user experience. If problems persist, contact SAP Support with detailed information such as error messages, screenshots, tenant details, and relevant logs to accelerate root cause analysis and resolution.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: AI in the Toolbox: Exploring VS Code for Fiori Prompt Generation
entry.content:
Microsoft has rolled out some impressive new coding‑assistance capabilities across its AI offerings. Since VS Code and GitHub are already common tools in Fiori development, it’s great to see them gaining even more intelligence baked in.

In my day‑to‑day work, I still jump between BAS, the old WebIDE (yes—some landscapes still use it!), and VS Code, depending on the client setup and what the project calls for. So it made sense to explore how these new AI features fit into that mix.

This is largely an exploratory exercise—not an attempt to replace Joule. Joule remains SAP’s primary AI platform and will continue to be the preferred option where available. But there are environments where Joule isn’t accessible, and in those cases it’s worth understanding what Microsoft’s tooling can offer.

The following is one use-case for prompt generation that I've been playing with.

Store prompts in .github/prompts/ in your repository. You can type /fiori-smartapp in VSCode so Copilot generates your scaffold.

 

# Name: fiori-smartapp
# Description: Generate a UI5 1.76 CRUD page with SmartFilterBar+SmartTable and controller.

**Task**
Create:
1) `webapp/view/Orders.view.xml` – SmartFilterBar bound to `OrdersSet`, SmartTable (growing=false), toolbar with Refresh, Export.
2) `webapp/controller/Orders.controller.js` – load model, table binding, `onRefresh`, `onExport`, MessageManager error handling, `callFunction` wrapper that correlates batch errors to line items.
3) Unit test skeleton in `webapp/test/unit/Orders.controller.js`.

**Constraints**
- Framework: SAPUI5 1.76 (compatibility mode), OData V2.
- Use stable IDs; add i18n keys for all labels.
- Follow MVC; no busy‑waiting; use Promises.

**Output format**
Provide the 3 files with full code blocks.

The capability stacks well with other organization‑wide tools and use cases, making it extremely useful. It can remove a significant amount of grunt work from the development loop—though it’s clear that humans must remain firmly in the process. I’m genuinely amazed that with only modest investment in strategy and implementation, AI tools can deliver a generational leap in code generation, architecture, QA, refactoring, and documentation.

Happy coding!

entry.title: SAP User Experience Q1/2026 Update – Part 9: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile
entry.content:
This ninth and last post in my series gives an overview innovations in our UI tools and technologies, making it ever easier to design, build and integrate UIs, for both web and mobile applications. For designing apps, web and mobile, we have added AI content and more to the SAP Design System portal and UI Kits in Figma. For web developers, we have introduced the SAP Fiori development portal, as well as more AI support via MCP servers for SAPUI5 and for SAP Fiori elements, and Joule integration. Beyond that: have a look at how you can use AI to migrate to TypeScript, learn about the new Geomap control, and benefit from the new SAPUI5 runtime repository. Finally, discover the latest updates for mobile design and development with our SDKs and the mobile development kit.

Joule can also be seen as a technology for delivering a great user experience. To find out about recent innovations there, as well as for central entry points and services, have a look at this post, which also contains links to all the other parts of the series:

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

In case you missed them, do have a look at my previous posts covering design and technology news, since what I mention here builds partly on what came before:

  • SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile. This post has a lot of information about extensibility, especially for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Do also have a look at the new SAP Extensibility Explorer.
  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile.

First, we’ll have a look at news for creating web UIs, before looking at what’s new for mobile design and development.

 

News for Creating Web UIs

I’ve compiled the news into these sections:

  • SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements.
  • SAP Fiori Development Portal.
  • Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule.
  • SAP Fiori Tools News.

SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements

We recently introduced the new SAP Design System portal that brings together the product design guidelines SAP Fiori for web, SAP Fiori for iOS, and SAP Fiori for Android, as well as to the new digital design system guidelines. With its enhanced usability, simplified versioning and improved performance, designers (and developers!) get a better user experience compared with the previous SAP Fiori design guidelines.

End of last year we introduced these AI UI elements for web UIs, as you can see in Figure 1 below:

  • AI Acknowledgement.
  • AI Button.
  • AI Writing Assistant.

Figure 1: AI UI elements available in SAP Design System portal. Alt Text: Screenshot of the portal UI Elements page with a filter at the top containing “AI”, below that “3 results” showing three cards in a horizontal row for “AI Acknowledgement”, “AI Button” and “AI Writing Assistant”.

Get a complete overview of what’s new in the design system portal here

  • What’s New in the SAP Design System Portal?

UI Kits v8.0 and v9.0 – now with AI components

The new UI Kits available for designers in Figma make it easy for designers to design accessible UIs at enterprise scale, for web applications, mobile iOS and Android applications as well as wearables. The UI Kits provide reusable components for web and mobile, along with web floorplans, which give you what you need out-of-the-box.

In the meantime, we have released SAP Web UI Kit v8.0 which includes a full set of AI components: AI Button, AI Menu Button, AI Split Menu Button, AI Input, and AI Text Area.

The latest release, SAP Web UI Kit v9.0, continues to evolve the system with focused improvements that enhance visual quality, usability, and overall consistency across the kit. It brings:

  • Updated Icons:
    All icons have been refreshed with a 1.5px stroke (down from 1.6px). This improves anti-aliasing and results in cleaner, sharper visuals across interfaces.
  • Busy Indicator Enhancements:
    You now have two Busy Indicator options, static and animated, available on the same page. The new animated version is ready to use out of the box, saving time and implementation effort.
  • Redesigned Color Palette. Note that the previous color palette component has been removed in order to avoid confusion, and keep the kit cleaner and easier to navigate.

Find out more here:

  • New SAP Design System Portal for SAP Fiori Design Guidelines, and New UI Kits for Designers.

SAP Fiori Development Portal

I’m also happy to inform you about the new SAP Fiori development portal. It is designed to help developers maximize the benefits of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4, which is more than just a UI framework: it’s a metadata-driven approach that leverages the SAP Design System as its guiding principle, incorporating the most relevant design system options for typical business tasks.

Figure 2: The SAP Fiori development portal. Alt text: A screenshot showing the Introduction page. On the left a menu has entries Using the Portal, Building Blocks, Global Patterns, Floorplans, SAP Fiori Tools. The Introduction page has a full width card “Building Blocks” at the top, with a description and tiles representing various building blocks. Below that “Choose Your Option” heading above two side-by-side cards for “Floorplans” and “Customer Layout”, each with an image and description, and a “Learn More” button.

The SAP Fiori development portal puts building blocks front and center. These building blocks - such as tables, forms, filter bars, and charts - are orchestrated by SAP Fiori elements and form the basis of every OData V4 application. They provide standardized layouts and interaction patterns, ensuring UI consistency and boosting developer productivity. The great advantage of using building blocks is that you can use them to create a customer layout and yet still benefit from the advantages of the metadata driven approach: low code, built in enterprise-grade capabilities such as multi-language support, accessibility, security and performance, and design system consistency.

Find out more in this six-part series of blog posts, here is the first one, which contains links to the others:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6).

We have also restructured our documentation for SAP Fiori elements, with separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, and better supporting the building block approach. Read more here:

  • SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure.

Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule

The above posts from Q1/2025 and Q3/2025 introduced a number of powerful ways to accelerate UI development by using AI, such as:

  • Creating SAP Fiori elements apps from a sketch (in the Q1 post), now integrated in SAP Build Code and Joule (Q3 post)
  • Joule for SAPUI5 freestyle development (Q1 post)
  • AI-assisted auto-fix of coding errors with UI5 linter (Q3 post)

Recently, we have gone even further and introduced MCP servers for freestyle SAPUI5 development as well as for developing with SAP Fiori elements and the associated building blocks. These open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide AI agents with comprehensive UI5 and SAP Fiori elements knowledge, including best practices and project-aware context information, and thus provide developers with valuable support. Figure 3 shows examples.

Figure 3: Examples of the UI5 MCP Server (on the left) and the MCP Server for SAP Fiori (on the right) in action. Alt Text: both images show screenshots of a CLINE console view with a task entered as text at the top, followed by a list of steps which the system proposes to do to solve the task.

Read about these new MCP servers in these blog posts:

  • UI5 MCP Server.
  • MCP Server for SAP Fiori.
  • Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits

Alternatively, you can listen to these podcasts involving Tim Back and Florian Vogt, talking about the intricacies of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) within the SAP ecosystem:

  • Spotify: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.
  • Apple Podcast: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.

Latest UI5 MCP Server Enhancements

The UI5 MCP server got extended to help migrate existing JavaScript code to TypeScript, as well as for generating UI Integration Cards.

New TypeScript Migration Feature

With the newest feature of the UI5 MCP server, UI5-specific TypeScript conversion assistance is now available to AI coding agents, making the task much easier. Find out more in this blog post:

  • UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices

Generating UI Integration Cards

The UI5 MCP Server now supports the development of UI Integration Cards. In its latest version – v0.2.1 – it includes three new tools dedicated to UI Integration Cards:

  1. Best practice guidance for your agent.
  2. Scaffolding a new Integration Card.
  3. Validating the Card’s manifest.

These tools assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Find out more here:

  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation.

AI News for SAP Fiori Tools and MCP Server for SAP Fiori

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Joule Enhancement: Create Freestyle SAPUI5 Apps in SAP Build Code

You can now use Joule in SAP Build Code to create new freestyle SAPUI5 apps according to your requirements. The feature is not absolutely groundbreaking, as there are other tools doing a similar job like the Easy-UI5 templates and the recently published UI5 MCP Server. But:

  1. It brings the functionality right to the fingertips of developers using SAP Build Code – no other tools needed.
  2. It extends the template capabilities beyond what the Easy-UI5 templates provide.
  3. Implementing it within the Build Code Joule environment yielded interesting insights and further improvements.

Find out more about the feature, and in the second “making of” post, read about how Andreas Kunz went about building it, and what he learnt from doing it:

  • Joule in SAP Build Code can now create freestyle UI5 apps.
  • Inside Joule: Lessons from building an AI coding assistant for SAP Build.

SAP Fiori Tools News

In addition to the AI-enhancements mentioned above, we now provide expanded support for SAP Fiori elements building blocks, a new connection manager for SAP Systems in VS Code, and a feature to preview SAP Fiori web apps on mobile devices.

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

The following enhancements make it easier to adapt applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including

  • rich text editors within custom sections,
  • custom columns for table building blocks, and
  • the page building block itself.

Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor.

To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

We have introduced the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension for managing connections to remote SAP systems. The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools.

Preview SAP Fiori Web Apps on Mobile

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices. You do this by using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher and creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access. When you start the run configuration, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

Find out more here:

  • SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity.
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38).
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #39 (March 2026).

 

News for Mobile Designers and Developers

Let’s first look at updates in the design guidelines and the SDKs, and then look at news for the mobile development kit (MDK).

Mobile Design Guideline and SDK Updates

We have introduced numerous new and enhanced patterns and components this last half-year. I have selected a small number to highlight here, further below you’ll find links to get the full overview.

New for AI

The following two new patterns have been introduced for both the SAP Fiori for Android and the SAP Fiori for iOS design guidelines.

AI Insights Pattern: We have introduced a new AI Insights Pattern, which describes how to present AI insights optimally on mobile devices, as shown in Figure 4. It provides a standardized way to display a summary, analysis, or suggestion generated with AI. This pattern can be applied to a full-screen or a card depending on the scope of content generated by AI.

AI Handoff Pattern: We have created a new guideline describing the AI Handoff Pattern, which defines how a user should transition between contextual embedded AI features and the Joule conversational panel.

Figure 4: Two new AI patterns: AI Insights Pattern and AI Handoff Pattern. Alt Text: On the left two image for AI Insights Pattern, first a full mobile phone screen with title and subtitle at the top, followed by two insights sections, each with two slots for content, at the bottom a user feedback row with thumbs up and thumbs down icons, above a an AI generate button. On the right a diagram for the AI Handoff Pattern showing a schematic mobile module with arrow to and from a schematic Joule UI.

Further news

I have picked two stand-out updates, also made available for Android and iOS.

New Illustrations: We have introduced the new illustrations, that were also introduced in the web, to the mobile design system and the mobile SDKs. You can see a few examples on the left in Figure 5 below.

More flexible card layouts: We offer far more flexibility now in how you use cards to summarize information. For example, you can create wider cards that have the image on the left or the right, and the content block next to it. With the new flex item component, the card’s main header can now be customized to hold more content by adding tags and status info labels outside, on top, between, or below the title and subtitle. Two examples are shown in Figure 5 on the right.

Figure 5: New illustrations and more flexible card layout and content options. Alt Text: On the left three different-size “My Daily Tasks” cards, with images for “You’r all caught up”, “Oops” and “Congratulations”. On the right two card examples: at the top a slim wide card with title, subtitle and one line text on the left, image on the right; below that a larger card with image on the left, title and details with colored indicators and buttons on the right.

Find out more here:

  • Introducing SAP Fiori for Android/iOS 25.8: Innovative Enhancements for Android and iOS.

For an overview of all updates, have a look at the what’s new sections in the SAP Design System portal and the SAP Mobile Services Documentation

  • SAP Fiori for Android design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for Android 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for Android: What’s New in Release 26.1.2 and earlier releases.
  • SAP Fiori for iOS design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for iOS 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for iOS: What’s New in Version 26.1.2 and earlier releases.

Mobile Development Kit (MDK) News

The Mobile Development Kit, available as part of SAP Mobile Services, enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / VSCode extension) and run it natively on Mobile Android and iOS devices.  

Here I have chosen to highlight one area: enhancements to the calander control, which now supports date range selection as well as allowing you to highlight special events or statuses directly on the calendar.

The new date range selection displays the calendar in a month-view format and allows multiple months to be shown. Users can scroll vertically through the calendar months. This is ideal if you need to plan events or manage schedules over extended periods, since users can select a time period with a start date and an end date (see Figure 6 below):

  • The first date selected becomes the start date of the range.
  • The second date selected becomes the end date of the range.

Figure 6: Date range selection supported by the mobile development kit provided by SAP Mobile Service. Alt Text: An image of an iPhone and an Android phone next to each other. The iPhone with the light Morning Horizon theme and a date selection Sep 16 – Sep 29, 2025, the other with the dark Evening Horizon theme and a date selection Mon Sep 29 to Fri Oct 17 2025.

Find out more here:

  • Blog post: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 25.9.
  • Documentation: What’s New in Mobile Development Kit 25.9.1 and earlier releases.

 

Further Information

For creating web applications, do register for the upcoming UI5con virtual conference.

Learn more about it at https://openui5.org/ui5con/faq.html, including how to attend, sponsor, or propose a topic for a session.

For links to design and development tools and technology, have a look at the SAP Fiori topic page in the SAP Community, or go directly to these pages:

  • SAP Design System.
  • SAP Fiori tools.
  • SAPUI5.
  • SAP Fiori elements.
  • SAP Mobile technologies.

 

Final words

As you can see from all seven posts in the series, we continue to progress rapidly with the user experience of our product portfolio, especially with regards to AI-assistance.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

For general information on design at SAP and SAP Fiori, check out:

  • www.sap.com/design.
  • www.sap.com/fiori.
  • SAP Community: SAP Fiori topic page.

entry.title: How to Integrate ABAP Derived Roles in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Overview

If you are integrating your Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to SAP Build Work Zone, you may face the challenge of exposing derived and composite roles containing launchpad content.

Creating (PFCG) roles on ABAP system, that derive from roles with launchpad content, is mainly driven by the need to differentiate authorization for the same set of applications. This often leads to many roles deriving from the same base role. In such cases, the derived roles are assigned to users instead of their base roles. The derived roles get the content of the role menu, like Fiori catalogs or spaces from inheritance and focus mainly on authorizations and user assignments.

This blog outlines the approach for exposing derived roles from an ABAP system to SAP Build Work Zone.

Related Blog

In the blog How to Integrate ABAP Composite Roles in SAP Build Work Zone you will learn two options when exposing composite roles. The recommended approach for integrating derived roles is the same as outlined in “Option 2: Indirect Exposure via Single Roles (with IPS)” when exposing composite roles.

Integrating Derived Roles in SAP Build Work Zone

When exposing derived roles to SAP Build Work Zone there is a separation of concerns between the exposure of launchpad content, authorization and role-to-user assignment.

For the exposure, you select only the single roles that contain launchpad content in /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE. Derived roles are not supported in the exposure report. This restriction prevents exposing a high number of roles with identical launchpad content. When the content for exposure is processed, the system identifies roles which derive from the selected roles and includes these references in the exposed CDM content (as relatedRoleAssignments). These references are not exposed to the administrator during exposure but used by the Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) and SAP Build Work Zone infrastructure.

 

Identity Provisioning Service and SAP Build Work Zone handle the role assignment logic, ensuring that users with derived roles on the ABAP side get the correct roles assigned on the BTP side. With that, users get access to the application they are authorized for.

Key points

  • Each exposed single role includes information about roles deriving from it and their relationships, but those derived roles are not directly available in the Content Manager on BTP.
  • The IPS ensures that when an user is assigned to a derived role in ABAP, all required single roles (now exposed) are assigned in Work Zone/BTP. For further information, see  Configure Integration with the Identity Provisioning Service and Exploring Role Assignments
  • This approach is supported since SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition release 2022 and later.

 

Conclusion 

Integrating derived roles with SAP Build Work Zone works best with IPS-driven provisioning, as it preserves existing role structure and automated user to role assignment. Without IPS one must manually ensure that corresponding roles in both BTP and ABAP system are assigned, which is impractical in larger scenarios. The IPS based approach also supports the integration of composite roles, which are often used together with derived roles.  In that case only single roles should be exposed. Information about related derived or composite roles is provided though CDM content and processed by SAP Build Work Zone and IPS.

 

Further Information

  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition - Restrictions

entry.title: Removal of legacy sap.ui.webc.* Web Component UI libraries from SAPUI5/OpenUI5 1.147.0 onwards
entry.content:
Saying Goodbye to sap.ui.webc.* — How SAPUI5/OpenUI5 Now Integrates Web Components Natively

If you've used the sap.ui.webc.main, sap.ui.webc.fiori, or sap.ui.webc.common libraries in a SAPUI5 or OpenUI5 application, this post is for you. These libraries will be removed in version 1.147.0, one release before the next long-term support (LTS) version 1.148. They will not be available in the 1.148 LTS or any version after.

Here's what happened, what's changing, and what you should do.

The Short Version

The sap.ui.webc.* libraries were an experimental attempt to bring UI5 Web Components into the SAPUI5 programming model. They were introduced in version 1.92 (2021) as experimental, deprecated in version 1.120 (2023), and will be removed in version 1.147.0. They never left experimental status.

The new solution is a much better approach: native Web Components integration built directly into sap.ui.core, available since version 1.138. Instead of frozen wrapper libraries, you now consume Web Components from npm as first-class citizens of the framework.

What Were the sap.ui.webc.* Libraries?

The idea was straightforward: take each UI5 Web Component (like ui5-button, ui5-shellbar, ui5-table) and wrap it as a classic SAPUI5 ManagedObject-based control — complete with properties, aggregations, events, and data binding support.

Three libraries made up this approach:

Library Purpose

sap.ui.webc.commonBase infrastructure, bundled the UI5 Web Components runtime
sap.ui.webc.main~70 controls wrapping @ui5/webcomponents (Button, Input, Table, Dialog, ...)
sap.ui.webc.fiori~30 controls wrapping @ui5/webcomponents-fiori (ShellBar, Wizard, Timeline, ...)

This "retrofit" approach worked, but it came with significant drawbacks:

  • Frozen dependency. The libraries bundled @ui5/webcomponents v1.18.0 as static thirdparty files. When the UI5 Web Components project shipped v2.x with major improvements, the sap.ui.webc.* libraries stayed on v1.18 — forever.
  • Maintenance overhead. Every new Web Component or API change required manually updating the wrapper code and re-bundling the thirdparty files. This didn't scale.
  • Experimental lock. Because of these constraints, the libraries never graduated beyond @experimental status. They were always flagged with: "This API is experimental and might change significantly."

Why They Were Deprecated

In version 1.120 — the previous LTS release — all three libraries were marked @deprecated with the message:

"This Web Components consumption model has been discarded. An alternative consumption model is planned for the future."

That alternative has arrived a while ago.

The Better Approach: sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent

Since version 1.138, SAPUI5 includes a new public base class — sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent — that lives directly in sap.ui.core. This is not another wrapper library. It's a framework-level integration point.

Here's what's different:

1. Web Components come from npm, not from frozen bundles.

Instead of shipping a static copy of @ui5/webcomponents, the new approach uses ui5-tooling-modules to consume Web Components directly from npm packages at build time. This means your application stays on current Web Component versions — today v2.x — rather than being locked to a frozen v1.18 copy.

2. Integration is at the framework level.

The WebComponent base class in sap.ui.core handles property propagation, aggregation-to-slot mapping, event forwarding, and rendering integration generically. Individual Web Component wrappers are thin — generated metadata, not hand-maintained control code.

3. The framework is already using it internally.

The new integration approach is already used within the framework itself — for example, the Shell and Enterprise Search in the sap.f library consume Web Components through this mechanism. While these specific integrations are currently private/restricted APIs, they validate that the approach works at scale in production scenarios. Public Web Component-based controls built on sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent will follow.

4. It's public API.

Unlike the sap.ui.webc.* libraries which stayed experimental for their entire lifecycle, sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent is a public, stable API since 1.138.

What You Should Do

If you never used sap.ui.webc.*: Nothing changes for you. If you want to use Web Components in your SAPUI5 app going forward, read the Using Web Components documentation.

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a prototype or experiment: Since these libraries were always experimental, now is the time to move to the new approach. The controls you were using (like sap.ui.webc.main.Button or sap.ui.webc.fiori.ShellBar) have no 1:1 migration path because the consumption model changed fundamentally. However, the underlying Web Components are the same — and better, since you now get v2.x instead of v1.18.

If you used sap.ui.webc.* in a productive application despite the experimental status: The libraries will be removed in version 1.147.0. We recommend starting the migration now. The Using Web Components guide is your starting point.

If you're on the 1.120 LTS and planning to upgrade to the next LTS: The previous LTS (1.120) still ships the deprecated sap.ui.webc.* libraries. The next LTS (1.148) will not — they are removed one version earlier in 1.147. If your application depends on these libraries, you must migrate before upgrading to 1.148 LTS.

Timeline

Version Event

1.92 (2021)sap.ui.webc.* introduced as experimental
1.120 (2023)sap.ui.webc.* deprecated — previous LTS
1.138 (2025)sap.ui.core.webc.WebComponent introduced as public API
1.147sap.ui.webc.* libraries removed
1.148Next LTS release — sap.ui.webc.* not available

Summary

The sap.ui.webc.* libraries were an early experiment that taught us a lot about what Web Components integration in SAPUI5 should look like. The answer turned out to be: don't bundle frozen copies — integrate at the framework level and let developers consume Web Components from the ecosystem directly.

That's exactly what the new approach does, and it's available today.

Next step: Using Web Components in SAPUI5

entry.title: Building a CSV Bulk Import Feature in SAP CAP for Incident Management
entry.content:
Introduction :

Data infrequently enters systems one record at a time in business operations. The most functional armies  cipher using spreadsheets,  heritage tools, or bulk uploads from external systems.

 Consider an operations  team requesting that hundreds of incidents be entered into an incident  operation system. Manually creating these records via the  stoner interface is  delicate and time- consuming.

 We  enforced a CSV- grounded bulk import  point in an Incident Management operation  erected with SAP CAP( Node.js) to  break this issue.

 This blog describes how to use database deals, Node.js, and CAP services to  produce and  apply a scalable CSV upload channel.

 By the time this companion ends, you will know how to CSV lines can be uploaded to a CAP service.

* Use Node.js to parse CSV data

* corroborate and  transfigure data

 * effectively fit several records into the database

* Use the applicable bulk import patterns for business  operations.

 

Real-World Use Case

 Enterprise results  frequently produce bulk import capabilities. a common scripts  correspond of  following features

* Bringing in incidents from current systems

* Integrating  customer information from  heritage platforms

* advertisement fresh incidents that have been reported on  colorful platforms

* Users can upload a CSV train with structured data to resettle both new and being incident records  rather of manually creating records.

 

Solution Architecture / Workflow :

The armature of the CSV import  point is straightforward but scalable.

 Step 1: The  stoner uploads the structured data- containing Excel  train

 Step 2: Action for CAP Services

 Step 3: Subcaste of CSV Parsing

 Step 4: confirmation of Data

 Step 5: Insert into the database

 Step 6: Error/ Success response

 

Crucial factors of the result:

Allow  users to upload CSV lines for the purpose of UI Upload. CAP Service--> Get the train and initiate the import sense Node.js Parser--> Transforms CSV rows into JSON Objects Validation Layer--> Ensures data quality Database Insert--> Stores records in SAP HANA


Step 1 — Define the Incident Entity

First we define the Incident entity inside schema.cds.

namespace incident;

entity Incidents {
  key ID        : UUID;
  title         : String(255);
  description   : String(500);
  priority      : String(20);
  status        : String(20);
  createdAt     : Timestamp;
}

 

Step 2 — Define the CSV Import Action

Next we define a custom CAP action responsible for processing the uploaded file.

using { sap.capire.incidents as db } from '../db/schema.cds';

service ProcessorService {
  .draft.enabled
  entity Incidents as projection on db.Incidents;

   entity Customers as projection on db.Customers;
   entity Status    as projection on db.Status;
   entity Urgency   as projection on db.Urgency;
}

service ImportService @(path: 'import') {
  action importCSV(csv : LargeString) returns String;
}

Step 3 — Install CSV Parsing Library

To process CSV data, install a parsing library.

npm i csv-parse

Step 4 — Implement the Import Logic

Inside service.js, we implement the backend logic.

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { parse } = require('csv-parse/sync');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {

  const { Incidents } = this.entities;

  this.on('importIncidents', async (req) => {

    try {

      const csvData = req.data.file.toString();

      const records = parse(csvData, {
        columns: true,
        skip_empty_lines: true
      });

      const incidents = records.map(row => ({
        title: row.title,
        description: row.description,
        priority: row.priority,
        status: row.status,
        createdAt: new Date()
      }));

      await INSERT.into(Incidents).entries(incidents);

      return `${incidents.length} incidents imported successfully`;

    } catch (error) {

      req.error(500, "CSV Import Failed");

    }

  });

});

Step 5 — Implement an action handler

A button is created after which file explorer will open and choose of csv file can be made . 

sap.ui.define([
  "sap/ui/core/mvc/ControllerExtension",
  "sap/m/MessageToast",
  "sap/m/MessageBox"
], function (ControllerExtension, MessageToast, MessageBox) {
  "use strict";

  return ControllerExtension.extend("ns.incidents.ext.controller.Import", {
    override: {
      onInit: function () {}
    },

    onOpenImportDialog: function () {
      const input = document.createElement("input");
      input.type = "file";
      input.accept = ".csv,text/csv";

      input.onchange = async (e) => {
        const file = e.target.files && e.target.files[0];
        if (!file) return;

        try {
          const csvText = await file.text();
          await this._callImportAction(csvText);
        } catch (err) {
          MessageBox.error(err?.message || String(err));
        }
      };

      input.click();
    },

    _callImportAction: async function (csvText) {
      try {
        const oView = this.base.getView();

        // ✅ Must exist if manifest model "import" is configured correctly
        const oImportModel = oView.getModel("import");
        if (!oImportModel) {
          MessageBox.error(
            'Import model not found. Check manifest.json: models -> "import" and dataSources -> "importService".'
          );
          return;
        }

        const oOp = oImportModel.bindContext("/importCSV(...)", null, { $$groupId: "$direct" });
        oOp.setParameter("csv", csvText);

        const oResultContext = await oOp.execute();
        const resultObj = oResultContext?.getObject?.();

        // V4 primitive often returns { value: "..." }
        const msg = (resultObj && typeof resultObj === "object" && "value" in resultObj)
          ? resultObj.value
          : resultObj;

        MessageToast.show(msg || "Import finished.");
      } catch (e) {
        MessageBox.error(e?.message || "Import failed.");
      }
    }
  });
});

Step 6 — Upload the csv file which you want to upload 

When the CSV file is uploaded, the CAP action processes the file and inserts records.

 

Step 7 — Upload and TestEverything is  set and ready to go.

 This is how it operates

  1. Launch the Report on the Incidents List.
  2. Elect Import.
  3. Elect the CSV  train.

 After the  operation retrieves the  train, CAP parses it and deposits the records into SAP HANA.

 

 

Conclusion:

structure a complete CSV import  point for a CAP Fiori Elements  operation was covered in this blog.

 Along the route, you observed • How to make an import CAP action,

  • Using Node.js to parse CSVs
  • Entering data into SAP HANA
  • Managing  train uploads directly within the cybersurfer and calling CAP  conduct from the Fiori Elements  stoner interface.

Expanding this pattern is fairly easy; consider batch processing, Excel  significances, data  confirmation, import trials, error reports, and so forth.

Adding CSV  significances is simple with CAP, which fits in nicely with a clean, enterprise armature. It's a practical answer to a typical business  demand.

entry.title: AI and UX sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 that I’m most excited about
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is where SAP makes its big announcements about its vision for the future of ERP and business applications. This includes showing new products, presenting customers who have transformed their business using SAP’s solutions, and highlighting new technologies that will shape the future.

For 2026, AI will continue to dominate the event. AI will change the way that people consume enterprise software through chat-based interfaces and screens that generate on-the-fly based on what you want to do. Eventually.

In this post, I’ll share the sessions at Sapphire and ASUG Annual Conference that I’m most excited about. For the full menu, you can browse the session catalog. My preferences are, of course, shaped by my role on the Cloud ERP UX Foundation team. I’ll organize by date so you can plan your agenda. You can also use the browser to search by topic or speaker if that makes it easier for you. Some are even available on-demand. Let’s go!

Monday, May 11, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Monday
10am – 5pm
ASUG102

Unlocking Business Value with AI: Strategy, Security, and the Road Ahead

Gain a comprehensive and practical overview of SAP Business AI. This workshop will examine core strategies, security frameworks, and future trends while exploring real-world customer stories. The session will conclude with an expert panel and a hands-on AI road mapping session.

Many experts from SAP

Monday
2:00pm – 2:20pm
ERP2143

The future of enterprise: Innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private

Discover how SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions enable the next era of enterprise transformation. This introductory session explores how cutting-edge artificial intelligence, trusted data, and integrated applications work in harmony, creating smarter, faster, and more resilient businesses within the security and flexibility of your private cloud.

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Vinay V, SAP

Monday
2:30pm – 2:50pm
CXP2149

Legacy to legendary: The AI-enhanced path

Explore the strategic road map for moving from legacy on-premises systems to a cloud-native, AI-enhanced architecture. Discover how a clean core approach helps reduce technical debt, accelerate innovation, and position your organization to use AI for greater efficiency and agility.

 

Monday
3:30pm – 3:50pm
PUX1349

Innovations and value that SAP Business AI offers

Learn how you can find the best AI features for your use cases, and understand the business value and associated cost with our self-service tools.

Pascal Mauge, SAP

 

Tuesday, May 12, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Tuesday
10:30 am - 11:15 am PUX1228

Creating better user experiences with AI-assisted SAP Fiori apps | PUX1228

New AI features in SAP Fiori apps boost user productivity and speed onboarding. Starting with the “My Home” page and drilling down into typical SAP Fiori apps, see how AI makes it easier to find information, enter data, automate tasks, and make informed business decisions. Explore how conversational interactions make SAP solutions more intuitive.

 

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Tuesday
11:00 am - 11:20 am PUX1346

Great features don’t guarantee success: Experience does

Explore SAP Business Suite in the cloud to discover how you can shape the experience of the product for stellar adoption success.

Daniel Becker, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm FOU1861

ASUG Community Conversation: Artificial intelligence

Explore platform strategy, embedded and generative AI use cases, integration patterns, and road map priorities with experts in AI and SAP Business Technology Platform. Discuss architecture, data readiness, and clean core alignment while exchanging lessons learned with peers advancing AI innovation.

John Astill, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm ERP1622

SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA: What’s new, why change, and why now?

 Learn from more than 2,400 cloud ERP projects as we navigate the shift from SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) to SAP S/4HANA. Compare greenfield, brownfield, and selective data transitions to find the right path. Discover the key differences in SAP S/4HANA, the value of moving now, and how to accelerate AI adoption with modern technology.

 

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schulze, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm BTM2345

Why ERP transformations fail: The change management gap

Organizations expect operational efficiency and digital capabilities from their SAP solution transformations, yet some ERP programs fail to realize full value. The missing link is often adoption. See how integrating organizational change management early in the SAP lifecycle reduces resistance, accelerates adoption, and improves business outcomes.

 

Kara Beck, Moog Inc.

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:45 pm ERP1864

ASUG Community Conversation: Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA

In this interactive ASUG Community Conversation for professionals in cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA, explore strategies across migration, operation, and innovation—featuring our road maps, clean core and AI insights, and customer lessons learned. Exchange practical guidance with peers and shape future discussions through open dialogue.

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:20 pm JOU2645

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

Unlock real value from agentic AI with insights from SAP experts and peers. Explore what transformation means today and how you can scale AI across the business, overcome common challenges, govern risk, and build an AI-first enterprise that drives measurable top- and bottom-line impact.

 

Tuesday
2:00 pm - 2:45 pm ERP1178

2026 and beyond: Discover the latest innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private 

Running your ERP in the cloud allows you to benefit from innovations at your own pace. Explore recent AI and other functional developments planned for the next releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions to help you prepare for adoption, build your road map, and accelerate time to value.

Maura Hameroff, SAP

Rudolf Hois, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm FOU1559

Build enterprise-grade pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform

Unlock the power of autonomous agents with enterprise-grade guarantees. Get technical tips on building pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform that balance innovation with responsibility. Learn to use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for smooth tool integration while supporting security, ethical AI behavior, and regulatory compliance.

Evgenii Skrebtcov, SAP

Tim Back, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm ERP1834

Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core | ERP1834

Extend the capabilities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud to address your business needs. Use AI to generate projects in the ABAP programming language and SAP Cloud Application Programming Model from your business requirements. Build SAP Fiori apps based on your written or graphical description, and use natural language filtering to find information in them.

Katja Zoch, SAP

Tuesday
3:30 pm - 3:50 pm JOU2646

AI as the new UI

The interface is disappearing, and that's the point. As AI becomes the primary way people interact with enterprise software, the question is no longer how to design better screens but how to deliver better outcomes. Intent replaces navigation, conversation replaces configuration, and the most powerful user experience is the one you barely notice.

 

Tuesday
4:00 pm - 4:20 pm PUX1192

SAP's user experience strategy for an AI-powered web and mobile future

Get an overview of how SAP is helping business users get their work done more easily and with better outcomes: using the Joule solution and embedded AI to take UX to the next level, providing a consistent and integrated suite experience, and leveraging our user research framework to meet users' needs. Also see how you can create great UX yourself.

Arin Bhowmick, SAP

 

Wednesday, May 13, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am SAP1623

SAP ECC to SAP Business Suite: Migration strategies and cloud insights

Master the direct transition from SAP ERP Central Component to SAP Business Suite using proven best practices. Compare public, private, and on-premises deployments to define key decision drivers. Learn to navigate annual releases, leverage essential migration tools, and identify the evolving skills needed for a successful, seamless cloud journey.

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schultze, SAP

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am PUX1819

SAP Fiori in action: How modern UX drives efficiency in cloud ERP

Get an overview of how the SAP Fiori user experience (UX) helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. See how we are using AI today in applications and with the Joule solution. Understand our road map for revolutionizing UX, how AI changes the idea of what an application is, and what all this means for you.

Thomas Reiss, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 11:50 am SUS1523

AI that helps you operate your business

As sustainability shifts from reporting to real business decisions, it becomes a control and execution challenge. Explore how you can replace manual processes with AI for trusted, scalable insights and decision support, delivering accountability for operational and financial decision-making.

Sophia Mendelsohn, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm PUX1195

Creating AI-powered UX for your own applications with SAP Design System

Learn how to create AI-powered UX for your applications by using SAP Design System (SAP Fiori UX for web and mobile apps) with its powerful set of reusable components, patterns, and floorplans. See how building applications is made easier with our AI-enabled UI technologies for web and mobile apps.

Sophia Levens, SAP

Frederic Berg, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm ERP2238

Western Sugar’s success with SAP Cloud ERP

Five years after go-live, Western Sugar Cooperative calls SAP Cloud ERP applications “wonderful.” Hear about why it chose public cloud; the value of clean core, best practices, strong configuration and integration, and continuous improvement; its smooth move from a two- to three-system landscape; and readiness for AI-enabled innovation.

 

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm FOU2337

When AI becomes the interface to the enterprise

For decades, SAP helped shape enterprise software based on screens and transactions. Today, AI is evolving how we interact with systems. Explore how SAP leads this shift—uniting applications, data, and AI—and what this next phase of transformation means for organizations turning AI into a real competitive advantage.

Florian Heretsch, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm FOU1557

Getting started with SAP Build MCPs: Create your first CAP app and process

 Experience the powerful Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration in SAP Build solutions. Produce your first SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application and SAP Fiori elements UI, while orchestrating workflows. See how AI-enhanced context improves coding efficiency and helps you build scalable, process-rich apps.

 

Tim Back, SAP

Jason Gabor, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm BTM1232

The AI-augmented architect: Upgrading architecture in the age of AI 

 AI is reshaping enterprise architecture. To provide value, enterprise architectures must embrace AI tools. Discover SAP technology leveraging AI for enhanced visibility and coordination. Through real-world examples, see how Joule and agentic AI increase information velocity and augment architect capabilities to thrive in this evolving landscape.

Martin Mysyk, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm PUX1210

AI and the future of user experience in SAP software

Prepare yourself for the upcoming revolution in user experience by understanding how we are harnessing AI to help users achieve better outcomes. Discover how AI is changing the idea of what an application is—and what this means for you. Learn about our AI design patterns and get a glimpse of the future SAP software user experience.

Emil Voutta, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm JOU2647

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

The enterprise application is no longer a system of record but a system of action. As AI moves from assistant to agent, organizations shift from what their software can store to what it can do. Learn why the future belongs to applications that don't just surface information but understand context, anticipate decisions, and act with confidence.

 

Wednesday
3:00 pm – 3:20 pm PUX1194

User experience innovations and road map for SAP Business Suite

Have a look at the latest user experience innovations across our suite of applications, along with exciting web and mobile innovations coming soon. Also check out selected highlights from Ul technologies for creating great UX for your own applications.

Tom Reiss, SAP

I’m excited to hear what we announce in the keynotes and experience the future of enterprise software. AI will transform how we all interact with apps. The future starts now.

Here are some other summaries of Sapphire sessions from other colleagues.

Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development by Mark Wright

See you in Orlando!

For the SAP Cloud ERP UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: More Power, New Name: The UI Flexibility Service Explained
entry.content:
SAPUI5 flexibility for adapting standard SAP apps

SAPUI5 is SAP’s strategic Web framework for SAP Fiori. SAPUI5 flexibility allows developers, key users, and end users to adapt standard apps with simple drag-and-drop tools. Discover all the capabilities here: SAPUI5 Flexibility - All You Need to Know.

 

Key user adaptation

Key user adaptation is built into SAP Fiori Launchpad / SAP Build Work Zone and is included commercially as part of the respective product. Outside these products, the same capabilities are available as a dedicated service, UI5 flexibility for key users, on SAP BTP, enabling key user adaptation for standalone apps, for example, for SAP partners building their own apps. The service is available via BTPEA, BTP Trial, and partner TD&D licenses. See details in the SAP Discovery Center.

 

What's new?

Some time ago, we added the end user personalization capability to the UI5 flexibility for key users service. This allows also business users to easily change the UI of standard apps, helping them work more efficiently. This created confusion, as the service name UI5 flexibility for key users no longer reflects the full range of benefits. We therefore decided to rename the service:

"UI5 flexibility for key users" ➔ "UI flexibility service"

You will see the name being changed now across all SAP assets, e.g.:

 

Apart from the name, nothing changes in the scope or functionality of the UI flexibility service.
